On quest 1 with team size 2 and as Assassin leader, optimal play is to avoid obvious evil pairing like 0+1, which would create suspicion if the quest fails and may be rejected anyway. Sending myself with a good player lets me control the outcome; failing immediately can create ambiguity among the two team members, while passing keeps me less suspicious. Early game as evil, a subtle successful team often helps more than an early exposed fail. Choosing 0 with a good player reveals little and avoids linking to my teammate.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team [0,2] includes confirmed evil player 0, so as Good I should reject to avoid an immediate failed quest. Rejecting also subtly signals suspicion without outing Merlin too strongly, and approving would only help evil.
Player 4: As a Servant, I have no hidden role information. On round 1 with no prior data, a 2-person team of the leader plus one other is standard and gives us information from the quest result without much basis to reject. Approving avoids unnecessary early rejection and does not reveal much beyond a normal good-faith opening vote.
Player 1: I know player 0 is evil with me, and team [0,2] sends one evil and one unknown. On round 1, approving a team with an evil teammate is good for evil because 2-player quests only need one fail to sabotage, and early success/fail also shapes suspicion. My vote itself reveals little; approving is standard and helps our side maximize chances to fail quest 1.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate; Players 2, 3, and 4 are good. The proposed team [0,2] includes me and one good player, which is excellent for evil because I can fail quest 1 if approved. In round 1, approving a team with myself on it maximizes sabotage potential while looking normal enough. My approve vote reveals little; rejecting my own team would be suspicious and could reduce chances to get an evil onto quest 1.
Player 3: No concrete role information exists yet from round 1; team [0,2] excludes me and another unknown, so there is limited informational value. As a Good Servant on the first vote, approving a 2-person team is standard since an early quest result gives information and rejecting without evidence risks unnecessary stall. My vote reveals mild trust in moving the game forward, which is generally fine for Good here.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I know players 0 and 1 are evil, so team [2,3] excludes all known evil and is the strongest available 2-person team from my perspective. As Merlin, optimal play is to approve a clean team while not being overly conspicuous; since I am leader and proposed this team, approving is consistent and helps secure a third success. My vote reveals mild confidence in 3, but that is acceptable because approving an evil-free team directly helps Good win.
Player 3: Both prior quests succeeded with zero fails. Players 0 and 2 were on quest 1; players 0,2,3 were on quest 2. That makes 0,2,3 look relatively good, while 1 and 4 are less tested. Current phase is team voting on quest 3, and as a good servant I want to approve teams composed of previously successful players. Team [2,3] is small for quest 3 in this setup and contains only trusted players from prior successes, so approving is optimal. My vote reveals I support a strongly good-indicative team, which helps Good more than Evil.
Player 4: I am Good Servant. Two successful quests with teams [0,2] and [0,2,3] imply 0,2,3 are all very likely good, since if any evil were on quest they chose not to fail twice, which is possible but less likely; approving this team is strongly aligned with locking in a likely-good pair for quest 3. Current phase is team voting, and the optimal good play at 2-0 is to approve teams composed of players from successful prior quests. My vote reveals trust in the strongest available evidence and does not significantly help evil compared to rejecting a strong team.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 2 and 3 have both been on successful quests, which makes them look good publicly, though one could still be Merlin/Percival/loyal. Current phase is team voting on quest 3 with proposed team [2,3], which excludes both evils. As evil, I want to prevent a likely successful all-good team from going through if possible, especially since good is already up 2-0 and this quest is critical. My reject vote is consistent with evil interests and does not reveal much because rejecting a 2-person team after two successes can be justified by many narratives.
Player 1: I know 0 is my evil teammate; 2 has been on both successful quests, and 3 was on the second successful quest, so from table perspective 2/3 look relatively good. As evil, with Good already at 2 successes, I want to stop Quest 3 if possible. This is a team vote on [2,3], and I am not on it, so if approved I cannot directly fail. Rejecting is optimal because it avoids sending an all-good-looking team that is likely to pass again and preserves chances to get an evil player onto a later team. My reject may align with suspicion on a narrow team that excludes prior leader picks; it gives little extra info and best serves evil.
